data.title: Fed's Bowman says job market may play bigger role in FOMC policy talks
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michelle Bowman expects that the job market and overall economic activity will play a greater role in the Federal Open Market Committee's policy debates, she said on Friday, addressing research presented at a conference exploring how monetary policy affects economic activity. 

"Although the FOMC has been focused on lowering inflation in the past few years, as we continue to make progress on approaching our 2 percent target, I expect that the labor market and economic activity will become a larger factor in the FOMC's policy discussions," she said in remarks prepared for delivery at a University of Chicago Booth School of Business event in New York.

Bowman did not reference Friday's nonfarm payrolls report, which showed still-solid job growth last month.

Elsewhere in her remarks, the Fed governor said that the so-called neutral rate, also known as R-star, — the rate that neither stimulates nor slows growth — likely increased since the pandemic. That implies that borrowing costs may need to remain higher than before to keep the economy in balance.

“One way to describe the resiliency of real activity to higher interest rates during the recent tightening cycle is to say that some of the previously noted factors led to a rise in R-star,” she said.

data.title: Traders move eye toward June rate cut by Fed after February jobs report
data.description:
Traders on Friday kicked up odds of a June interest-rate cut by the Federal Reserve after markets received an overall stable U.S. jobs report for February. 

Traders in the fed futures market raised June rate-cut odds to 53.7% from 47.5% a day ago, according to the CME FedWatch tool. The May probability stepped down to 41.5% from 46.5% on Thursday. 

The “employment report highlighted the obvious in that the US economy is indeed slowing on the margin, but more importantly, we are not seeing the economy fall off a cliff,” Charlie Ripley, senior investment strategist at Allianz Investment Management, said in a note. 

“Overall, this report is a sigh of relief for the Fed as they can continue to sit on the sidelines for the next couple meetings as they assess the potential inflation impacts resulting from US tariff policy over the coming months,” Ripley said. 

Nonfarm payrolls rose 151K in February vs. 160K consensus. The unemployment rate edged up to 4.1% vs. 4.0% consensus. A cooler 0.3% increase in average hourly earnings matched the consensus. Federal employment was down 10K as U.S. President Donald Trump’s appointed DOGE team directed workforce cuts. 

“The Fed is in a pickle,” Comerica Bank Chief Economist Bill Adams said. “On the one hand, slower job growth and rising unemployment are arguments for a rate cut. On the other hand, inflationary policies like tariffs and immigration restrictions are arguments against a cut,” he said.

“Most likely the Fed will sit on their hands for a bit, just like private hiring managers seem to be doing," Adams said. 

In markets Friday, Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.23%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 4 basis points to 3.91%.

U.S. stocks (SP500)(NYSEARCA:SPY)(COMP:IND)(DJI) fell, with equities heading for weekly losses.

data.title: Eli Lilly posts long-term data for eczema therapy Ebglyss
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) on Friday said that its recently approved eczema therapy Ebglyss led to complete skin clearance in 50% of individuals who took part in a long-term extension study.

Citing data from ADjoin, a long-term extension study of the company's Phase 3 Ebglyss trials, the Indiana-based drugmaker said that 50% of trial subjects met the highest bar of therapeutic response, showing complete skin clearance as measured by a clinical score known as EASI 100.

Meanwhile, 87% achieved almost-clear skin (EASI 90) at three years. As for tolerability, the company said that the safety findings were in line with those from Ebglyss trials.

Those who responded to Ebglyss at Week 16 from the company’s ADvocate 1 and ADvocate 2 monotherapy trials took part in Adjoin. They received biweekly or once-monthly Ebglyss as maintenance therapy at a 250 mg dose.

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) develops the injectable in partnership with Spanish biopharma company Almirall (OTCPK:LBTSF). In September, the FDA approved Ebglyss for use in those aged 12 years and older with moderate-to-severe eczema (atopic dermatitis) after topical prescription drugs.

data.title: BofA’s Hartnett: Buy lagging India stocks
data.description:
BofA analysts recommended investors to buy lagging international markets (EEM), especially India (BATS:INDA), (PIN), (FLIN).

Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA, pointed out that India’s small-cap stocks (SMIN) have been in a bear market for the past six months.

They are down 21% from September 2024’s peak.

 

 

 

India (BATS:INDA) has seen equity outflows of $3.1B year-to-date.

In addition, Indian equities have seen the largest year-to-date underperformance among international equities, down 9.2% in U.S. dollar terms.

They were also the lowest ranked international equities in deviation from their 200-day moving averages in U.S. dollar terms, down 11.7%, the most oversold equities globally.

data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb gets European Commission nod for Opdivo plus Yervoy in liver cancer treatment
data.description:
• Bristol Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) said on Friday that it received European Commission approval for Opdivo plus Yervoy for the treatment of liver cancer.
  • The approval came after results from late stage trial showing a statistically significant improvement in overall survival with Opdivo plus Yervoy compared to lenvatinib or sorafenib.
  • In addition to approval in HCC, Opdivo-based options are also approved for treatment of multiple tumor types in the EU, the company added.

data.title: China’s ‘Fab 4’ are outperforming the U.S.’s ‘Lagnificent 7’ – BofA
data.description:
The launch of DeepSeek’s open-source AI model R1 triggered a $3T market cap loss to $15T for the “Magnificent Seven” stocks, said Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA, in a note.

Most of these “Lagnificent Seven” stocks, as he calls them, are down, except for Meta Platforms:

  • Tesla (TSLA) – -35.05% year-to-date
  • Nvidia (NVDA) – -16.38% year-to-date
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) – -8.84% year-to-date
  • Amazon (AMZN) – -8.55% year-to-date
  • Meta Platforms (META) – 7.16% year-to-date
  • Microsoft (MSFT) – -6.92% year-to-date
  • Apple (AAPL) – -4.88% year-to-date

On the other hand, the market cap of China’s “Fabulous Four” – Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent, and Xiaomi – doubled to $1.6T.

  • Alibaba Group (BABA) – 69.88% year-to-date
  • Xiaomi Corp. (OTCPK:XIACF) – 57.92% year-to-date
  • Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY) – 29.07% year-to-date
  • Baidu (BIDU) – 13.24% year-to-date

Nonetheless, equity inflows into U.S. equities (DJI), (SP500), (COMP:IND) continue to dominate. The U.S. saw $123B of inflows into equities year-to-date. China (MCHI), year-to-date in general, saw $14.9B of outflows.

But on the 200-day moving average basis, China equities are the highest ranked in deviation, up 20.3%, while U.S. equities are up 2.2% from 200-day moving averages.

Lastly, Hartnett said that European equities (VGK) are “not too far behind China” this year, which he named “year of international,” with longs in China and Europe.

More China ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (CQQQ), (FXI), (GXC), (MCHI), (FLCH), (CNYA), (ASHR), and (YINN).

Magnificent Seven related ETFs: (BATS:MAGS), (MAGX), (QQQU), and (QQQD).

data.title: Gap's comeback gains steam as Dickson's playbook delivers results
data.description:
Gap’s (NYSE:GAP) Q4 top and bottom-line beat coupled with above-consensus comparable store sales for 3 of its 4 banners not only broke a six-day losing streak for the stock, but will force investors to re-evaluate the future of this once-anemic brand, and it’s standing in the fickle retail sector.

“Not only did Q4 results offer further evidence that brand reinvigoration/turnaround strategies are proving successful, but they also make for a consistent performance track record under new management,” Morgan Stanley’s Alex Straton said.

Gap CEO Richard Dickson certainly gets much of the credit for the brand’s newfound relevance. Since taking the helm of Gap (NYSE:GAP) in August 2023 after his successes with Barbie, Hot Wheels, and Fischer-Price, Gap (NYSE:GAP) has beat EPS estimates in every quarter since and reached its highest gross margin in 20 years.

“We believe Mr. Dickson’s prior experience in reinvigorating brands provides GAP with a head start in both formulating and implementing its playbook…therefore, we expect investors to increasingly focus on Mr. Dickson’s approach to improving brand relevance the company’s overall product offering,” Jefferies’ Corey Tarlow adds.

With a double-digit gain at Friday’s open, the better-than-expected results helped offset what is considered conservative guidance.

“We’ve been operating in a highly dynamic backdrop for the last few years, and we’re expecting the same for fiscal 2025. As a result, we’ve taken a balanced view with our guidance and remain focused on controlling the controllables,” CFO Katrina O’Connell said on the earnings call.

Gap’s (GAP) guidance “embeds headwinds that other Specialty peers’ guidance may not fully contemplate,” Straton notes, including tariff headwinds even though Gap (GAP) sources less than 1% of their merchandise from Mexico and Canada. With the inclusion of minimal FX headwinds, Gap’s (GAP) guidance sets the stage for another beat.

There are, however, a few speed bumps Gap (GAP) faces in the near term, including lingering sluggish sales at Athleta, Old Navy (which drives 60% of the company’s revenue) losing its current growth trajectory, and momentum at Banana Republic stalling.

But for Dickson, these issues are not insurmountable, as the company is now building on a much stronger foundation.

“This year represents an exciting step in our ongoing transformation, as we begin to transition our focus from fixing the fundamentals to continuous improvement through innovation and pave the way for momentum in the years ahead.”

data.title: Biggest stock movers: AVGO, LUNR, ZIM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower on Friday as investors awaited Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s speech, following a weaker-than-expected rise in nonfarm payrolls. Market sentiment remained cautious, with volatility driven by tariff's policy uncertainties and trade tensions.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers

Biggest stock gainers

  • Walgreens Boots (NASDAQ:WBA) +5% – Shares gained after private equity firm Sycamore Partners agreed to acquire the company for $10B. Sycamore will pay $11.45 per share, with shareholders potentially receiving an additional $3 from proceeds tied to the sale of Walgreens' primary-care assets, including VillageMD. The total transaction value could reach $23.7B. Expected to close in Q4, the deal includes a 35-day “go-shop” period, allowing Walgreens to seek alternative offers. Sycamore has secured fully committed financing, and the transaction is not subject to a financing condition.
  • Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) +13% – Shares soared after the semiconductor and software giant posted strong FQ1 results and guidance, reinforcing that AI-related spending remains robust. CEO Hock Tan highlighted continued strength in AI semiconductor revenue, projecting $4.4B in Q2 as hyperscale partners ramp up investments in AI XPUs and connectivity solutions for AI data centers. For Q2, Broadcom forecasts revenue of approximately $14.9B, surpassing the $14.59B consensus, with adjusted EBITDA expected to be around 66% of total revenue.
  • The Gap (NYSE:GAP) +18% – Shares jumped as the retailer surpassed Q4 estimates, fueled by better-than-expected comparable sales in three of its four brands and its strongest gross margin in two decades. Despite the strong results, Gap issued cautious guidance for the current quarter, citing macroeconomic uncertainty and potential effects of U.S. trade policy, though less than 1% of its merchandise comes from Mexico and Canada. For Q1, net sales are projected to be flat to slightly higher than Q1 2024’s $3.4B, with a modest Y/Y gross margin increase from 41.2%. In FY25, net sales are expected to rise 1%–2% to $15.25B–$15.40B, in line with the $15.38B consensus, while operating income is forecast to be between $1.20B and $1.22B.
  • ZIM Integrated Shipping (NYSE: ZIM) +7% - Shares soared amid takeover speculation, following reports that CEO Eli Glickman is exploring a management-led buyout of the Israel-based shipping firm. According to a StreetInsider report citing an unidentified source, Glickman is working with advisers on a potential bid, though it remains unclear whether his efforts will materialize or if the board will be receptive. The company is set to report its Q4 earnings on Wednesday.

Biggest stock losers

  • Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) -20% – Shares plunged after the company posted mixed FQ1 results and issued a weaker-than-expected outlook. HPE also announced a cost-reduction program, including a workforce reduction of up to 3,000 employees. For the current quarter, the company expects adjusted EPS between $0.28 and $0.34, significantly below the $0.48 consensus, with projected revenue of $7.2B–$7.6B, missing the $7.94B estimate. For FY2025, HPE forecasts adjusted EPS with a midpoint of $1.80, falling short of the $2.12 consensus.
  • Intuitive Machines (NASDAQ:LUNR) -34% – Shares plunged after reports of complications with its IM-2 mission Nova-C lunar lander, Athena, despite maintaining a signal with the spacecraft. The company has been reallocating power to critical systems to address unexpected positioning issues on the moon, raising investor concerns about the mission's success and its broader implications. With a press conference scheduled for later today, the market awaits further details on Athena’s status and next steps. The setback has dampened optimism about Intuitive Machines’ space exploration capabilities, fueling uncertainty about its valuation and future prospects.

data.title: Airbus says January-February deliveries down from year ago
data.description:
Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) reported Friday that deliveries for January and February fell 18% Y/Y to 65 aircraft, including 40 jets handed over to customers in February, lagging behind the same two-month period in 2024 when it delivered 79 aircraft.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) had warned about relatively weak deliveries in this year's Q1 after supplies from engine maker CFM were pulled forward to last year's Q4 to help the planemaker to broadly reach its annual target.

The company also said it secured just 14 new orders for February, all for the A321neo, bringing the YTD total orders to 69; after cancellations, net orders in the first two months reached 65, up from 33 at the same point last year.

When Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) unveiled FY 2024 results, which included a net profit of €4.2B, the company guided for 2025 deliveries of ~820 aircraft, compared to 766 planes that were handed over in 2024.
